our_argument_summary: Section 3.5.6 argues that the dome's headline '95.2% Accuracy' is (a) hardcoded static HTML with no programmatic derivation, (b) based on an editorial denominator choice that excludes failures, (c) inconsistent with the repo's own API data (96.3%, 97.0%), and (d) inflated by non-independent predictions. The section checked every plausible source file and found no script computing the figure.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: The 95.2% figure appears as static HTML on the dome's homepage. No script in the dome's repository computes an aggregate accuracy percentage. The dome's various pages report inconsistent counts (homepage: 67 confirmed/0 falsified; tracking page: 53 confirmed/4 falsified; scorecard API: 26/27; results API: 33/33).
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The 95.2% is hardcoded. Our review correctly identifies this.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None identified for this specific claim.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review's core claim — that no script computes 95.2% — appears well-supported. However, see holes_found for a math error in our own rendered text.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: This is a section-level review, not a WIN. The 95.2% claim is a static HTML string on the dome homepage; no monitor.py domain validates it.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: N/A — section-level item, not a physical phenomenon claim.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The 95.2% figure is retroactively computed from known WIN statuses, not a forward prediction.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The accuracy percentage has no derivation from dome geometry — it's a count of self-declared 'confirmed' predictions divided by a chosen denominator.

holes_found.description: MATH ERROR IN OUR OWN RENDERED TEXT. Section 3.5.6 uses the JavaScript template variable ${counts.total} in the sentence: 'The model page now displays the arithmetic ${counts.total} / (${counts.total} + 4) = 95.2%'. When counts.total = 67 (current WIN count), this renders as '67 / (67 + 4) = 95.2%'. But 67/71 = 94.37%, NOT 95.2%. The equation 95.2% requires approximately 79-80 WINs in the numerator. A dome defender reading our review would immediately spot that we printed an equation that doesn't balance — undermining our credibility on the very section where we criticize the dome for innumeracy. Either: (a) we should use the dome's actual numbers from their page instead of our ${counts.total} variable, or (b) we should note that 67/71 ≠ 95.2%, which is itself another layer of the dome's math error.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the template variable with the dome's literal numbers as they appear on their page. If the dome's page literally says '67 / 71 = 95.2%', then quote it verbatim and add a note that this is arithmetically wrong (actual = 94.4%). If the 95.2% dates from an earlier WIN count, say so explicitly. Do NOT render an equation with our variable that produces the wrong answer.

holes_found.description: SAME MATH ERROR IN ALTERNATIVE CALCULATION. The text also says '${counts.total} / (${counts.total} + 4 + 4 + 1) = ${counts.total}/76 = 88.2%'. With counts.total = 67, this renders as '67 / (67 + 4 + 4 + 1) = 67/76 = 88.2%'. Checking: 67/76 = 88.16%, which rounds to 88.2% — this one is actually correct. However, the denominator construction (67 + 4 + 4 + 1 = 76) needs scrutiny: the '4' for below-detection, '4' for falsified, and '1' for removed. This math checks out.
holes_found.recommendation: The 88.2% calculation is arithmetically correct. No change needed for this specific equation.

holes_found.description: AMBIGUOUS CITATION: '(W001, W004)' FOR 4 ITEMS. The text says the formula 'excludes 4 as "below detection threshold" (W001, W004)'. But W001 and W004 are only 2 identifiers. If the dome excludes 4 predictions, we should name all 4. If only 2 are excluded, the denominator should be +2, not +4. The dome's V50.6 page says '2 Below Detection', which aligns with 2 items, not 4. A dome defender would say we inflated the denominator to make the accuracy look worse.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify whether V51.0 has 2 or 4 below-detection predictions. If 2, the dome formula should be 67/(67+2) = 97.1% (or whatever their actual claimed formula shows). If 4, name all 4 predictions. The '(W001, W004)' citation is ambiguous — it could be read as 'W001 through W004' (4 items) or as 'W001 and W004' (2 items).

holes_found.description: UNVERIFIABLE CITATION: '4 falsified predictions (W019, W020, W024, W027)'. Our text claims 'the model's own tracking page acknowledges at least 4 falsified predictions.' The V51.0 raw text directory is empty (no extracted content), so this claim cannot be verified from our archived source material. The V50.6 raw text shows W019-W022 as PENDING weekly tests, and the homepage claims '0 Falsified'. Section 3.5 references a V51.0 tracking page with 4 falsified and 53 confirmed, but this isn't in our raw-text archive.
holes_found.recommendation: Archive the V51.0 tracking page content to raw-text/v51.0-2026-04-05/ so this citation is verifiable. If the tracking page has been taken down or changed, note when it was observed. The claim may be accurate based on live observation, but it's currently unfalsifiable from our archived data.

holes_found.description: SECTION MISSING FROM DOCX. A grep of build-doc-v4.js found no match for '95.2', '3.5.6', or 'accuracy claim'. This section appears to exist only in the HTML output, not in the downloadable Word document. Since the DOCX is presented as a parallel document, this omission means the DOCX lacks one of our stronger code-analysis arguments.
holes_found.recommendation: Add section 3.5.6 to build-doc-v4.js to maintain parity between HTML and DOCX outputs.

holes_found.description: The argument that 'many WINs are re-sliced versions of the same data (WIN-040 through 043)' is strong but could name additional examples. WIN-045/046/049/050/051 (five tidal periods from the same Doodson/IHO tables) are arguably even more egregious — they are known constants, not measurements requiring a model.
holes_found.recommendation: Add the tidal period cluster (WIN-045/046/049/050/051) as a second example of non-independent prediction inflation.

stronger_arguments.description: The 95.2% figure doesn't even survive its own arithmetic. If the dome claims 67 WINs and 4 below-detection, that's 67/71 = 94.4%, not 95.2%. The headline number is wrong by its own formula. This should be stated explicitly — it's a devastating detail that the accuracy claim can't even do the division correctly.

The version evolution page (06-evolution.txt) shows the dome's scorecard evolving from 'Dome 5, Globe 50' (V0.4) to 'Dome 26, Globe 0' (V49.2+). The Globe column went from 50 to 0 — not because the globe model got worse, but because the author removed the comparison. The accuracy metric is meaningful only in comparison to an alternative; removing the comparison column is itself an editorial choice that inflates perceived accuracy.

The dome's own AI context page (07-ai-context.txt) reports a DIFFERENT scorecard: '39 Confirmed, 2 Below Detection, 19 Pending, 0 Falsified' (total denominator = 60). That gives 39/41 = 95.1% (if counting only confirmed/below-detection) or 39/60 = 65.0% (if counting pending as not-yet-confirmed). The 95.2% doesn't match this breakdown either. Every internal data source gives a different number.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model does maintain a public predictions registry with timestamped, falsifiable claims — this is more than most fringe models do. The concept of tracking an accuracy percentage against registered predictions is scientifically sound methodology. The act of registering eclipse predictions with git SHA timestamps shows genuine engagement with falsifiability principles.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The methodology is sound in principle but the execution is fatally flawed: the numerator counts non-independent, post-hoc, and trivially confirmable claims; the denominator is editorially chosen to exclude failures; and the resulting percentage isn't even computed by any code — it's typed into the HTML. Good methodology applied dishonestly is worse than no methodology, because it creates a false appearance of rigor.

deeper_analysis: The critical finding is that our own review contains a math error that mirrors the dome's error. We use ${counts.total} (= 67) in a formula and claim it equals 95.2%, when 67/71 = 94.4%. This is ironic — we're criticizing the dome for hardcoding an inaccurate number, while our template-generated text produces an inaccurate equation. A dome defender could quote our own rendered HTML ('67 / (67 + 4) = 95.2%') and say 'They can't even do basic division while lecturing us about math.' This must be fixed before any other changes. The fix is straightforward: either quote the dome's literal numbers (whatever appeared on their page when 95.2% was accurate), or explicitly note that 67/71 ≠ 95.2% as yet another error in the dome's claims. The stronger approach is the latter — it adds another data point to our argument that the headline is unsupported.

citations_verified: commit 5c00275 referenced for V51.0 registry lock — referenced in our text, not independently verified but consistent with CLAUDE.md version history
citations_unchecked: scoring.js content claims (we describe its behavior but the dome repo files are not in our raw-text archive)
api/scorecard.json giving 26/27 = 96.3% (cited in our text, not archived)
api/current/results.json giving 33/33 = 97.0% (cited in our text, not archived)
